content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 8, 1992


Bruce Robeck
Nielsen, Merksamer, Parrinello,
Mueller & Naylor
770 L Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Request for Confirmation
     of Telephone Advice
     Our File No:  I-92-231

Dear Mr. Robeck:

     You have requested confirmation of advice provided to you
regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act
("Act").1/  Since you have not identified the person on whose
behalf the request is being made, we provide the following
informal response to your question.2/

QUESTION

     Must a lobbyist employer disclose payments for research
that is conducted as part of a company product development and
sales program, if the results of the research are used in
presentations to administrative agencies?

CONCLUSION

     Payments made for research programs are not reportable if the
programs are incurred for purposes other than to influence
legislative or administrative action.




_________________________
1/   Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/   Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Robeck, Bruce
May 8, 1992
Page 2


FACTS

     You requested advice on behalf of clients that are lobbyist
employers and engage in product development and sales marketing.
Specifically, you asked if payments for research programs which
produce data on product safety, environmental impact, packaging
and market appeal are reportable if a company subsequently uses
the research data in presentations to California governmental
agencies. You stated the research programs are not initiated for
the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action.

ANALYSIS

     The Act requires lobbyist employers to file periodic
statements disclosing payments made to influence legislative or
administrative action.  (Sections 86115, 86116.) A payment to
influence legislative or administrative action is defined in
Section 82045 to include:

     ...(b) Payment in support or assistance of a
lobbyist or his activities, including but not
limited to the direct payment of expenses incurred
at the request or suggestion of the lobbyist...

     In addition, a payment made to influence legislative or
administrative action is further clarified in Regulation 18616.
Subsection (a) provides that payments in connection with the
following types of activities must be reported:

     (A)  Support or assistance of lobbyists;
     (B)  Direct communication with a legislative,
agency or elective state official for the primary
purpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action;

     (C)  Soliciting or urging persons other than
the filer or the filer's employees to enter into
direct communication with a legislative, agency or
elective state official for the primary purpose of
influencing legislative or administrative action.

     Further, subsection (f)(2)(c) requires disclosure of:

     Payments of any other expenses which would not
have been incurred but for the filer's activities
to influence or attempt to influence legislative or
administrative action...

     If a company's product development research is not incurred
for the purpose of assisting lobbyist activities and the research
would have been incurred regardless of the company's activities to
influence or attempt to influence legislative or administrative action, the research
expenditures are not required to be disclosed on a Report of

Robeck, Bruce
May 8, 1992
Page 3


Lobbyist Employer (Form 635). This is true even if the company
later uses the data in presentations before California
governmental entities. However, although the research data costs
are not reportable, publication expenditures (e.g. brochures and
charts) prepared and developed for the purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action are reportable.

     Please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5662 if
you have additional questions concerning this matter.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel

                                   By:  Lynda Cassady
                                   Political Reform Consultant
